data.title: SCHG: The 'Good' Giant Is About To Become Appealing Again
data.description:
Today, with so much discussion about potential turbulence ahead in the market, it is time again to talk about the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF™ (SCHG). In my last coverage, right after liberation day, there was a lot of panic in the financial markets, and through an evaluation process that I invite you to read, we had estimated a target price of about $28, with an upside of 14%.

Well… we went far beyond what seemed fair. In fact, the most widespread concern among analysts today is the overextension of big tech, a sector in which SCHG is, as we have seen, naturally and for obvious reasons, rich.

The question we ask ourselves today, following the same approach/methodology, is: is SCHG still a hot pick? Before answering, here are some things you absolutely need to know.

A necessary TL;DR

To fully understand what we are talking about, I invite new readers to follow me and take a look at this TL;DR on SCHG’s characteristics:

  • the ETF has an AUM of $54.241 billion, particularly pronounced.
  • It has an expense ratio of 0.04%, very low even for a passively replicated ETF.
  • It has one objective: to track as faithfully as possible the total return of the index, before fees and expenses: Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Growth Total Stock Market Index, which selects U.S. large-cap growth with growth criteria well defined in the prospectus.
  • SCHG continues to maintain added value compared to peers, as highlighted in the last coverage, and well represented by the SA risk grade table that I share with you.

The holding distribution

The concentration within the top 10 exceeds 58%, and the first 3 stocks alone (Nvidia + Microsoft + Apple) represent over 30.89%. In fact, the weighted average market cap is $1,899.558 million, so SCHG is structurally an AI-driven ETF, not just growth.

At the sector level, there is unsurprisingly a strong prevalence in the tech segment, deriving from a sectorial distribution as follows:

The holdings distribution is rebalanced on a quarterly basis, with a monthly recheck, to avoid violating concentration limits.

Valuation

Having established that the ETF is qualitatively distinctive, the question that now naturally arises is: “how are these valuations sustainable?” Let’s follow the reasoning that proved successful in the last article, and start by calculating, based on updated FactSet data, the expected EPS growth (as a weighted average of the expected 2026 EPS growth rates of the sectors inside SCHG). A value of 17.80% emerges. Interesting, because it is still higher than the expectations in April, which were around 14%.

At this point, trying to work with expectations, we need the estimate of the fwd P/E; and by replicating the same approach, we obtain a forward P/E of 23.86x, naturally higher than the old value of 22.2x from April 2025.

What I think, and what I share again today, is that if the forward P/E is too far above the 50th percentile, SCHG is expensive, but if it drops toward the 50th percentile, the valuations become acceptable. If we think about it, how can the market afford to let the valuations of a book of stocks with a 27% ROE and 5Y EPS growing at 30% fall below the historical average? It would even be strange if it didn’t appreciate a premium in my opinion.

And indeed, from my latest personal estimates, today we are roughly close to a 74th percentile, therefore, above the historical average.

Operational risk

Unpopular opinion: I think a premium is natural with these expectations. Similarly, at the operational level, I would prefer to consider SCHG again at prices about 7% below current levels, therefore under the $30 mark. Among other things, a point at which, with equal expected EPS, the relative valuations would return closer to average values, still at a premium (around the 60th percentile).

It should be considered, in my honest opinion, that a market at a premium remains a market at a premium: meaning it remains more vulnerable to risks. Read here for further insight.

Conclusion

I therefore think that the right rating for SCHG today is Hold. This is because, although the price drop has given some breathing room to relative valuations, the market still remains at a premium. And attention: I am of the opinion that a premium here is more than justified by the ROE; but the way I work, I would prefer to see the ETF’s price closer to the $30 level. But I remind everyone, it remains a personal opinion.

data.title: Metropolitan Bank: Momentum Wanes As The Stock Becomes Fully Valued
data.description:
Just five months after my last coverage, Metropolitan Bank Holding Corp. (MCB) has already shown some interesting changes. Its value reached its 12-month high of $80.78 before correcting to its current price of $72.95. This justifies my bullish view before. Today, it is still well-positioned with its robust performance and disciplined loan management. However, it’s slightly challenging to take a long position at this level. The stock is still reasonably priced but fully valued. Technicals are still bullish but show some weaknesses.

MCB Q3 2025: Sustained Strength Amid Macroeconomic Volatility

As a bank, Metropolitan Bank Holding Corp. is highly cyclical and vulnerable to macroeconomic volatility. Inflation remains stubborn, while the Fed has implemented a series of interest rate cuts in the past year. Yet, the bank continues to show resilience with its prudent asset management and diversification.

During Q3 2025, its total interest income amounted to $132.0M, up by 9.6% YoY from $120.5M and by 3.9% QoQ from $127.0M. This also marked its rebound from the relative weakness in Q1 2025. Although interest income still increased, it shrank to 5.7%. This can suggest that MCB has already coped with the policy easing cycle. Note that the Fed slashed the interest rate by 100 bps during the second half of 2024. This affected the yields of variable-rate loans, deposits, and investment securities in Q1 2025. But this also led to an influx of loan demand as volume increased. On a YoY basis, net loans have already increased by over $800M or 14.6%. Its well-diversified investment securities also helped increase asset yields.

Meanwhile, its interest income remained flat and stable, which suggests prudent financing strategies by the company. It also benefited from the policy rate cuts because it lowered yields on deposits. As a result, its net interest income remained high at $77.3M up by 18.6% YoY. It only had to increase its provisions to cover more risks amid its loan base expansion. Even so, MCB was still profitable.

Risk Factors

Inflation is still a big risk factor for banks like MCB. Currently, it’s at its 14-month high of 3.0%. It remains stubborn, so macroeconomic conditions remain volatile and even gloomy. New tariffs must also be considered due to their inflationary nature. It can lead to higher import costs, so businesses may have to pass on the added costs to consumers. This can affect the fiscal and monetary decisions of policymakers. Hence, MCB should stay on the watch and be more prudent with its loan management and investment diversification strategies.

Mitigants/Drivers

Despite these challenges, I believe that MCB is well-positioned.

1. Loan Management Is Well-Positioned Against Macroeconomic Volatility. One aspect to consider is its loan diversification. MCB has a very high exposure to commercial loans, which comprise over 90% of the total amount. One-to-four-family real estate loans and consumer loans comprise the remaining 2%. In my view, this is strategic for MCB because commercial loans are more stable than consumer loans. Borrowers are typically businesses, so they have more means to repay their loans and avoid delinquencies and/or defaults. In addition, these are typically collateralized, which makes them more secure than consumer loans.

Moreover, I like how it splits its loans between fixed and floating or variable rates. Its Q3 2025 SEC filing is not detailed, so I had to trace it back to the 10-K filing of Q4 2025. As you can see, the portion of fixed-rate loans maturing after a year is 82% versus floating-rate loans with 18%. This positions MCB very well for the policy easing cycle. Why? First, only a minimal portion of the total loans will be subject to repricing. So, the yields of fixed-rate loans will not decrease even if the rate cut continues. Second, yields are more protected from macroeconomic volatility. They remain stable even if monetary policies become erratic. Third, this allows the bank to lock in higher margins. After all, having more fixed rates amid policy easing means manageable yields with decreasing funding costs.

2. Its Prudent Asset Diversification Offers Flexibility. As a bank, it’s normal for MCB to capitalize heavily on loans. But I like that its percentage of the total interest-earning assets remains reasonable at 81%. Investment securities comprise 14% of the total. These are mainly debt securities, so risk exposure is manageable. It also has government-backed securities, which are typically inflation-linked. This can help stabilize investment yields. So, it’s not surprising that interest income on securities rose by $600,000, or 12.8% YoY.

3. Well-Capitalized Business Means Safety From Financial Crises. Its highest-quality capital or CET1 Ratio is 10.6%, which is enough to cover that portion of risk-weighted assets. If we add the other tier 1 capital and the remaining ones, the total capital ratio will be 12.2%. This is still higher than the minimum required ratio of 8.0% by Basel III. So, it can withstand a financial crisis and remain liquid without resorting to borrowing. This allows it to limit the risk of deposit runs and survive even if it happens.

Valuation

After revisiting its valuation using book value, the price increase from my previous coverage is justified. Using P/B, MCB trades at 1.03x its book value versus the five-year average of 1.02x. More or less, the stock is still trading within the average range. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP or target price will be $71.88. With that, the stock is still reasonable. However, it is already fully valued, which also justifies the recent pullback. Its upside potential is already limited, so entering or increasing your position at this price may not be wise.

Technicals

MCB’s technicals are still bullish, but it has weakened recently. The price shows rebound attempts from the pullback as it increases and coincides with the 50-day SMA. This means that the current price trend or momentum is still as strong as the average in the past 50 days. Meanwhile, the 50-day SMA is still above the 200-day SMA, which means that the average price trend in the past 50 days is still higher than in the past 200 days. However, it has already started to trend downwards, which suggests a negative shift in market sentiments.

Its MACD and RSI can explain it. The MACD line is now trending upward after crossing above the signal line, which means increasing buying volume. The histogram is back to positive territory, which implies that buying volume has already outpaced selling volume. Meanwhile, RSI shows that the uptrend was due to the recent overselling. Now, it’s getting closer to being overbought again. So, momentum may start to weaken again. Eventually, profit-takers may act again and this could lead to a dip.

Conclusion

Metropolitan Bank Holding Corp. remains a solid bank with its robust performance and fundamentals. Its prudent loan and investment diversification positions it very well against macroeconomic headwinds. However, valuation shows limited upside, so entering at this current price may not be strategic. Technicals are still bullish, but the waning momentum verifies the idea that the stock is already fully valued. I advise investors to stay on the sidelines and downgrade my rating from buy to hold.

  • The fund employs a team of portfolio managers, with each member dedicated to one or more of the major market sectors, plus a group leader who is responsible for general team oversight, cash management and risk monitoring.
  • Portfolio sector weightings are kept similar to those of the index in an effort to add value through active stock selection – our core competency – and also to minimize the risks associated with sector or market timing.
  • Focused sector expertise, supported by our deep research infrastructure, is combined with disciplined portfolio construction to provide investment-process consistency in seeking to deliver attractive risk-adjusted returns over time.
  • Our sector-based structure preserves individual creativity and accountability, core to Fidelity's investment culture.

For definitions and other important information, please see the Definitions and Important Information section of this Fund Review.

Performance Review

For the three months ending September 30, 2025, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 3.70%, trailing the 5.55% advance of the benchmark S&P MidCap 400® Index.

In the third quarter, U.S. stocks extended the rally that began in April following the 90-day pause for most tariffs. Mid-caps trailed both large-caps and small-caps, with micro-caps turning in especially strong performance the past three months. Likely, this was triggered by a growing consensus that the U.S. Federal Reserve would cut its key policy interest rate in September, which did, in fact, occur.

Lower interest rates are viewed as particularly beneficial for smaller businesses, which have access to fewer ways of raising working capital. At the same time, large-cap equities were propelled higher by strength in the "Magnificent Seven" group of Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon.com (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA) and Tesla (TSLA).

Within the mid-cap benchmark, communication services (+21%) and information technology (+12%) were the only two sectors to record a double-digit gain, although utilities (+9%) and health care (+8%) came close. Within communication services, the media & entertainment segment (+36%) stood out to the upside among all the benchmark's industries. On the other hand, only consumer staples (-6%) stocks posted a decline for the quarter, notably weighed down by household & personal products (-16%) firms. In a mostly risk-on quarter, investors tended to shun more-defensive segments of the market, including staples stocks. Other sectors with lagging performance included materials (+1%), consumer discretionary (+2%) and energy (+4%).

Turning to the portfolio, security selection among industrial stocks, especially in the capital goods industry, notably detracted from performance versus the benchmark. Picks in health care and information technology also hurt.

Among individual positions, out-of-benchmark exposure to The Baldwin Group (BWIN) weighed on the fund's relative result most this period. Shares of the insurance distribution firm returned -34% for the three months amid mixed Q2 financial results. While earnings met expectations and revenue exceeded the consensus estimate – driven by strong organic growth – certain segments faced challenges. Specifically, growth was flat in the Mainstreet Insurance Solutions segment, and the Medicare business experienced headwinds due to disruption in the managed care landscape. We opportunistically added to the position in Q3.

Negligible exposure to Comfort Systems USA (FIX) also proved detrimental. The stock gained 54% for the quarter, fueled in part by the company's surprisingly strong quarterly earnings report in July. For Q2, the provider of mechanical and electrical services saw its earnings grow about 35%, notably exceeding analysts' consensus expectation. Revenue eclipsed $2 billion for the first time, propelling the exceptional quarter that also was aided by new business from technology clients, a large percentage of which was tied to the build-out of data centers. This was a newly added holding this period.

An overweight in Kyndryl Holdings (KD) was another negative, given its roughly -29% result the past three months. Spun off from IBM in 2021, Kyndryl is the world's largest provider of information technology infrastructure services to businesses. The shares fell sharply on August 5, the day after the company reported mixed quarterly financial results. While profitability was stable, revenue fell 5%, which was short of consensus estimates and down from last year's Q2, when adjusted for currency fluctuation.

Conversely, investment choices in the financials and consumer discretionary sectors contributed to performance versus the benchmark.

In stock-specific terms, a non-benchmark stake in The Bancorp (TBBK) topped led the way. Shares of the regional lender, based in Delaware, recorded a roughly 31% gain for the quarter. The firm has been reporting accelerating revenue for four quarters now, while earnings growth has been solid. In Q2, earnings grew 21% and total revenue rose 10.8%. Although earnings were a bit shy of analyst expectations, revenue beat the consensus estimate. The Bancorp was the portfolio's largest holding at the end of September.

Elsewhere, Western Digital (WDC), another non-benchmark position, surged about 87% the past three months and added value compared with the benchmark. In late July, the data-storage company reported notably better-than-expected quarterly revenue and earnings, fueled largely by favorable supply-and-demand dynamics in the hard-disk-drive industry. Management noted strong demand from its cloud/hyperscale customers amid tight supply.

Outlook and Positioning

Given ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty, the co-managers continue to favor high-quality businesses capable of delivering growth through strong market positions, idiosyncratic drivers or cyclical recovery.

In the third quarter, the stock market's rally appeared to be broadening out, although it did not benefit mid-caps as much as we would have liked. If both these trends continue, we expect the mid-cap group to participate and perhaps even take a turn at propelling equities higher. With that said, we do not consider market forecasting one of our core strengths. Rather, we concentrate on building the portfolio one stock at a time, based on bottom-up, company-level factors.

Overall, we remain confident in the fund's investment process, in which shareholders are supported by a team of sector-focused co-managers charged with adding value through stock selection, and a group leader who is responsible for general team oversight, asset allocation, cash management and risk monitoring.

At quarter end, none of the portfolio's sector weightings diverged materially from those of the benchmark. This is in line with our broadly sector-neutral approach that seeks to add value mainly through security and industry selection.

The fund's most prominent out-of-benchmark positions as of September 30 were The Bancorp (TBBK), Howmet Aerospace (HWM) and HEICO (HEI), the latter two being suppliers of parts and components for the aerospace industry. Among benchmark holdings, XPO (XPO), Brinks and Wesco International (WCC) were the most sizable overweights at the end of Q3.

XPO is a leader in less-than-truckload freight transportation in North America. Brinks a global provider of cash and valuables management, digital retail solutions and ATM managed services.

Wesco International provides electrical, industrial and communications maintenance, as well as supply-chain management and logistics services.

On the other hand, the fund's three largest individual underweights at the end of the three-month period were Pure Storage, Flex and Comfort Systems USA, all benchmark components we avoided due to some combination of unsatisfactory growth prospects and elevated valuations, in our view.

CHARACTERISTICS

Earnings-Per-Share growth Trailing measures the growth in reported earnings per share over trailing one- and five-year periods.

Earnings-Per-Share growth (IBES 1-Year Forecast) measures the growth in reported earnings per share as estimated by Wall Street analysts.

Median Market Cap identifies the median market capitalization of the portfolio or benchmark as determined by the underlying security market caps.

Price-to-Book (P/B) Ratio is the ratio of a company's current share price to reported accumulated profits and capital.

Price/Cash Flow is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months cash flow per share.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio (IBES 1-Year Forecast) is the ratio of a company's current share price to Wall Street analysts' estimates of earnings.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio Trailing is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months earnings per share.

Return on Equity (ROE) 5-Year Trailing is the ratio of a company's last five years historical profitability to its shareholders' equity.

Preferred stock is included as part of each company's net worth.

Sales-Per-Share growth measures the growth in reported sales over the specified past time period.

Weighted Average Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the average equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

Weighted Median Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the median equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

The highest (or most favorable) percentile rank is 1 and the lowest (or least favorable) percentile rank is 100. The top- performing fund in a category will always receive a rank of 1%. % Rank in Morningstar Category is based on total returns which include reinvested dividends and capital gains, if any, and exclude sales charges. RELATIVE WEIGHTS

Relative weights represents the % of fund assets in a particular market segment, asset class or credit quality relative to the benchmark. A positive number represents an overweight, and a negative number is an underweight. The fund's benchmark is listed immediately under the fund name in the Performance Summary.

3-YEAR RISK/RETURN STATISTICS

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (less) than 1 is more (less) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R- Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

data.title: SCHG: Capture The Growth Of AI With Less Volatility
data.description:
Overview

The majority of my posts are centered around dividend growth, but investors shouldn't forget that growth is an important piece of the wealth building formula. This is why I often manually reinvest dividends into growth positions across my portfolio. While I typically opt for individual positions, I do hold many growth ETFs throughout different retirement accounts. Having exposure to a high quality growth ETF gives instant exposure to some of the highest quality businesses in the world. I believe that the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF™ (SCHG) has proven to be one of the most efficient choices out there.

When looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that SCHG has provided a total return of 18.25%. Since SCHG's starting dividend yield is relatively low at 0.3%, most of this return has come from the fund's price growth. SCHG captures this growth by placing a primary emphasis on technology stocks, which have historically increased their earnings more rapidly than other sectors. With technology being the greatest focus area, this means that SCHG is naturally exposed to many of the companies leading the artificial intelligence charge forward. Therefore, SCHG is a great option for investors that are bullish on the outlook of AI.

When I previously covered SCHG, I issued a Buy rating and went into the details on how it compared to the Vanguard Growth Index Fund ETF (VUG). However, I wanted to revisit SCHG and put together a more dedicated article on the fund's strengths and weaknesses. There are some clear benefits that SCHG offers, as well as some clear risks that investors should be aware of. SCHG has an inception dating back to December of 2009, meaning we have nearly two decades worth of operating history to reference. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that SCHG implements.

Less Volatile Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, SCHG has total net assets of $50.7B that is spread across 195 different positions. The fund offers a straightforward way to get exposure to some of the fastest growing companies in the world, in a cost-efficient manner. For instance, the fund has a very reasonable expense ratio of 0.04%. While SCHG doesn't aim to mimic any specific index, it does focus on large-cap U.S equities that exhibit growth style characteristics.

Looking at the fund's breakdown by sector, we can see that Technology companies make up 48% of total assets. This is followed by exposure to Communication Services and Consumer Discretionary investments, which accounts for 14% and 13% of assets respectively. I believe that two notable aspects of this breakdown is how it results in an excellent Sharpe ratio and Standard Deviation over the last three years.

  • Sharpe Ratio: 1.68x
  • Standard Deviation: 16.34%.

The Sharpe ratio is basically a measurement of how much excess return the fund provides per unit of risk/volatility. Typically, anything above a 1.5x is great. For reference, the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) has a Sharpe ratio of 1.43x. Additionally, the Standard Deviation is a measure of the overall volatility of its holdings. For reference, the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) has a Standard Deviation of 16.78%, meaning that SCHG is a bit less volatile than QQQ.

Since the fund is focused on Technology companies, the top holdings come as no surprise. For instance, Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest position at 11.55% of assets. This is followed by weight in Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), Broadcom (AVGO), and Tesla (TSLA), just to name a few. However, SCHG does lean a bit top heavy since its top ten holdings make up over 58.5% of total assets. This leaves SCHG vulnerable to sharp downside movements within the tech sector. So despite the fund offering exposure to nearly 200 companies, SCHG's sense of diversity is a bit misleading.

AI Momentum Without Concentration

The biggest argument for maintaining exposure to SCHG is due to the exposure to companies that are paving the path forward within the AI space. NVDA is the clear leader in accelerated computing and supplying chips across the industry. Then there's the other Mag 7 companies like Alphabet (GOOG), which are leading the way with its software, such as Gemini. Companies like AAPL, AMZN, and Meta Platforms (META) are all finding creative ways to implement this technology into their everyday ecosystems to improve user experience, functionality, and quality of life. The point being, SCHG provides exposure to the many companies that have the most resources to continue growing the utility and profitability of AI.

I believe that we are still at the beginning phase of this AI growth and SCHG is one of the easiest ways to capitalize on this. For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI market can increase to ~$3.5T by 2033. This represents a massive CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 31.5% from 2025 through 2033. Even in a scenario where this research is overstating the growth by 10%, we're still looking at massive growth over the next decade. With so many companies trying to carve a path forward, selecting individual stocks can be challenging since the tech industry changes so rapidly. This is why the simplicity of SCHG is great for investors that simply want a piece of the action by implementing a buy-and-hold strategy.

By taking an ETF approach, you are also eliminating the single-stock risk of the sector. While I am optimistic on the growth of the AI sector, I think the path forward will be filled with uncertainty, volatility, and competition. For instance, META recently confirmed that they plan to spend $600B in capex to build out AI infrastructure in the US. As extreme levels of capital flow into the building of this infrastructure, this leaves investors curious if this large level of spending is really necessary. Market sentiment in reaction to these sort of headlines can quickly cause momentum to shift to the downside.

Risks

Since the fund is heavily concentrated to the top ten positions, SCHG has demonstrated that it can expose investors to greater downside risk during market declines. The most recent example can be demonstrated when measuring the performance earlier in 2025. Market indices pulled back from their highs in response to uncertainty around President Trump's tariffs.

The chart below is dated from the beginning of the year until the market bottom on April 8th. According to the performance graph below, we can see how SCHG experienced a slightly larger downside compared to both SPY and QQQ over the same time frame.

Therefore, SCHG may not offer any resilience during a prolonged decline. If the markets suddenly become less optimistic about the growth, capabilities, or utilization of AI, the positive momentum throughout the sector may fade. Additionally, many of SCHG's top holdings are priced at very high premiums at the moment. For instance, NVDA trades at a large price to earnings ratio of ~44x. Instead of parroting many of these same concerns, I would suggest giving fellow analyst, Phillip Brohl's, article a read. Phillip has issued a sell rating with the basic premise arguing that many of the fund's holdings are trading at unjustified valuations.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on SCHG at this time. A position in SCHG makes the most sense for investors that are bullish on the outlook of the AI sector. Data suggests that the size of the AI market can increase at a CAGR of 31.5% through 2033. SCHG provides instant diversity across many of the highest quality businesses that are leading the charge forward. Additionally, SCHG has demonstrated that it can provide attractive returns, while maintaining a lower Sharpe ratio than some more traditional index-tracking ETFs. Therefore, SCHG is a great for investors that want a piece of the growth in a cost-efficient manner. However, the focus on technology means that SCHG is also vulnerable to downturns in the technology sector.

data.title: Bullish: As BTC Prices Collide, This Investment Is Stuck In The Penalty Box
data.description:
With all the focus planted on year end stock market volatility, investors should also pay close attention to the warning signals being flashed in the crypto markets. For me, the eroding price of Bitcoin isn’t just asset-specific fear about the so-called four year halving cycle, but a general risk-off attitude that is entering into the mainstream as we head into 2026.

Bitcoin’s sharp drop has taken down a number of crypto stocks, whether or not they are directly impacted by the decline of BTC. Put Bullish (BLSH) in the “heavily impacted” category. The exchange operator will be hit by trading volumes plus a devaluation of its own balance sheet. Already down more than 50% from post IPO highs around $90, Bullish has more to lose.

I last wrote a sell article on Bullish in October, when the stock was trading in the mid $50s. The stock’s collapse since then has not induced me to buy the dip just yet, as I think the loss of value is commensurate to the company’s own sustained losses on its crypto holdings, plus the slowdown in its trading and CoinDesk businesses. I’m reiterating my sell rating here.

Poor indicators through Q3 and beyond

Let’s first start with a discussion of how Bullish’s core business has weakened before diving into its balance sheet erosion as well. Bullish just reported Q3 results that beat out Street expectations on growth, but overall showed worrying trends. Take a look at the key trended metrics in the chart below:

As a reminder, Bullish has two principal sources of revenue. It operates CoinDesk.com, which is a crypto news and market cap tracking site. It also opened a new subscription-only research service, akin to a paid equity research platform for the crypto industry.

We note as shown in the chart above that Bullish’s monthly active user counts fell -29% y/y to 2.7 million; which also reflects a sequential loss of 0.2 million subscribers. This is a critical source of subscription and advertising revenue that stabilizes Bullish’s more choppy trading platform.

On the other and more prominent side of the house, trading, we note that Bullish has executed its long awaited entry into the U.S. market on October 31, which happened after the close of Q3.

Like other exchanges, Bullish reports its trading statistics on a monthly basis. Spot trading volumes in October nearly doubled month on month to $77.5 billion ahead of the U.S. launch. Though Bullish only guides to subscription and services revenue and doesn’t attempt to guess at trading volumes, we do expect a sharp surge in Q4.

Speaking to the launch trends on the Q3 earnings call, CEO Tom Farley noted as follows:

Our exchange launched in full and without risk caps at the tail end of October. In just over 2 weeks, we've already traded well over $1 billion of volume. And as of today, we have approximately $1 billion in open interest. Our best day was yesterday, where we traded $240 million, about 4% market share by our definition. I'm really excited by the traction we've attained right out of the gate, and I expect it to become a significant contributor to our financial performance going forward. Look, I've been involved with a lot of these derivatives launches over the years, including very successful ones and a few that I rather not discuss. This one has all the hallmarks of a big winner.”

That said: the expectations of the launch were already baked into the share price. Exclude the impacts of the U.S. launch, and trends might be much worse. In Q3 (through the month of September), I’m concerned about the fact that average trading volume of $1.42 billion fell -21% sequentially versus Q2.

We might get a temporary lift in Q4 when U.S. trading kicks in, but once the U.S. launch is fully comped, we may start to get a feel for “organic” growth which may be a lot weaker. We note that Bullish faces no shortage of competition, with much larger-name brokerages like Robinhood (HOOD), Coinbase (COIN), and Binance dominant in the U.S.

Balance sheet devaluation

To some extent, a lot of the value in Bullish was also in its crypto treasury strategy, and its fortunes rose and wavered with the health of the crypto markets. Unfortunately, the recent slide in Bitcoin prices does a lot of damage to Bullish.

Relative to peaks above $125k, Bitcoin has sank ~30%. There has been a big splash of recent headlines about the driving causes behind this that we won’t rehash here, but the most prominent is the fact that Bitcoin prices have typically peaked in four year cycles, and investors are fearing another repeat of 2022, where BTC fell from above $60k at 2021 highs to briefly dip below sub-$20k.

As shown in the chart above Bullish reported $3.49 billion of net liquid assets on its most recent balance sheet, which is a substantial chunk of the company's current $5.89 billion market cap. To a large extent, an investment into Bullish is necessarily tied to the value of its crypto portfolio.

The snapshot below, meanwhile, showcases the company's principal holdings. Of its $4.35 billion in digital asset balances (excluding debt, which is what its net liquid assets represent), $2.76 billion is invested into Bitcoin, and $89 million into Ethereum at market prices of $113,074 and $4,160 respectively.

As of the time of writing, relative to Bullish's mark-to-market balance sheet as of Q3, the BTC on Bullish's balance sheet has fallen -23% to $113.1k, while ETH has taken an even sharper -30% tumble to $2,930. This equates to a loss of $634 million on Bitcoin, and $27 million on Ethereum.

The fall in crypto markets may have more adverse impacts beyond simply devaluating Bullish's balance sheet. Fewer investors may be trading in and out of Bitcoin as it falls, especially given the "HODL" mantra among many crypto enthusiasts, which may impact Bullish's trading volumes amid the U.S. launch.

Valuation and key takeaways

Despite all of its headwinds plus its recent correction, Bullish doesn't necessarily trade at a discount to peers, either. As shown in the chart below, Bullish sits at a ~70x forward adjusted EBITDA multiple, which is well ahead of Coinbase (COIN) at ~21x forward adjusted EBITDA.

I think it's important for investors to allocate some direct exposure to crypto (particularly BTC) in our portfolios, as well as to invest in companies that are innovating in payments and the blockchain. But my preferred investments in this space are Circle (CRCL), which is the sponsor behind #2 stablecoin USDC, and Figure Technologies (FIGR), which operates a blockchain lending platform that can tokenize and collateralize real-world assets like real estate to originate loans, principally HELOCs. Both trade at cheaper multiples than Bullish, and don't have the same exposure to falling crypto prices (especially Circle, where USDC price doesn't fluctuate at all, but whose yields are tied to the Fed's decisions on short-term interest rates).

Resist the temptation to catch a falling knife here.

data.title: Workday Q3 Earnings: Lacking Confidence In The Execution Of The AI Growth Story
data.description:
Introduction

Workday (WDAY) just reported its Q3 earnings that beat on top and bottom lines with decent growth numbers, but when looking at the report in more detail, we can see some issues appearing. I’ll go through the numbers in more detail and give some comments on the company’s strategy, which I think is not strong enough for it to convince me to open a position.

Since the last time I covered the company, back in August ’24, the company’s shares performed quite badly, losing 20% of their value compared to the S&P500’s (SPY) 20% appreciation.

By the Numbers

Starting from the top, the company’s sales came in at $2.43B, up 12.5% y/y, and just beat analysts’ estimates by $10m. I’d call this meeting estimates. Looking at the sales numbers in more detail, Subscription revenues were $2.24B, the majority of sales, increased by 14.6%, and professional services came in at $188m, down 6.5% y/y. This segment continues to decline and will most likely disappear soon, especially since it is making an operating loss this quarter.

Speaking of profitability, Q3 non-GAAP EPS came in at $2.32, beating estimates by $0.15. The company’s operating margins across the board improved to 10.7%, on a GAAP basis. The company’s relatively high share-based compensation accounted for another 15.5% in loss of GAAP operating margin, so adjusting for that, the company’s non-GAAP operating margin came in at 28.5%, up 220bps due to SBC declining 150bps to 15.5%.

Let’s look at the company’s financial position. WDAY ended the quarter with $2.6B in cash and equivalents, as well as $4.2B in marketable securities against around $3B in long-term debt. This position continues to be solid. The debt on the books is not significant given the company’s operating performance and a massive cash pile.

On top of these results, the company provided us with a look at the following quarter’s performance. Subscription revenues are expected to be around $2.355B, which represents 15.5% y/y growth, with a non-GAAP operating margin to be at least 28.5%. For the full year, the company expects to see subscription revenues reach $8.828B, which is 14.4% growth y/y and a non-GAAP operating margin of 29%. These numbers are not far off from what analysts were expecting the company to achieve for the rest of the year, but it seems it wasn’t good enough, since the company’s share price initially went up on the double beat, but has dropped 10% on the open.

It all came down to what the analysts heard during the conference call. At first sight, the company’s numbers looked solid, but as the conference call went on, analysts saw that a lot of this perceived growth was in fact inorganic through mergers and acquisitions. The company’s current remaining performance obligations came in at a decent growth, but only due to the recent acquisition of Paradox, which added over a point to the cRPO number. Without it, and adjusting for short-term tenant impacts, the growth actually decelerated. The same situation regarding the next quarter, too. The increase in $20m to cRPO for Q4 is due to the recently closed Sana acquisition.

So overall, the company looked decent at first glance, but once everyone started to dig into the report more closely, the company’s performance was not due to organic growth, but rather through M&A strategy, which isn’t ideal. It is still growing, but it begs the question of how sustainable such a strategy is going to be in the future.

Comments on the Outlook

So, it seems that the company’s share price was essentially priced to perfection, and what looked like a decent report was nowhere near enough to save it from the plummet this morning. So, what is there for the company to do to get back onto the better side of the investing community?

AI continues to be the company’s growth opportunity

Workday also announced recently that it is going to open a new AI Center of Excellence in its EMEA headquarters in Dublin, Ireland, creating 200 specialized AI roles. WDAY continues to depend on the AI growth story and is looking to expand its role in any way it can. The company’s expansion and deepening adoption of AI-powered products such as Workday Go, Extend Pro, and other AI SKUs, tells us that AI adoption is a major strategic pillar.

The company is shifting towards an open, partner-driven AI ecosystem called the Workday Economy, which aims to foster innovation and customer engagement through AI-powered automation and Agentic AI capabilities. Agentic AI is becoming the new innovation within such companies, helping enterprises increase efficiency throughout their operations, which seems to be working quite well; however, it is far from perfect, but with time, it will save a massive amount of capital for enterprises.

The company continues to innovate, or at least that is what the financial position tells us, since most of the share-based compensation is for product development. This is a good sign that the company is trying to keep ahead of the competition, and now that most of the companies involved in any sort of way with AI reported increases in capex for next year, there is a good chance that the company will continue to ride the AI growth story wave a little longer, which will most likely translate into a recovery in the company’s share price. The company has continued to release hundreds of new features over the last few years, including AI-enhanced talent management and finance automation tools, so at that rate, there will be some feature that is going to stand out among the rest that will most likely drive the company’s subscription base higher. And I’m sure it will be something to do with AI.

What I am not a fan of

In the past, the company got away with inorganic growth, but it seems to be catching up to it right now. People are seeing that this may not be sustainable in the future. Granted, it has a lot of money, so right now, that is not going to be an issue for the next few years at least, but what happens after that? Are we going to see the company’s top-line performance dwindle to low single-digits, or even decline? That is a possibility, especially when the demand for everything related to AI starts to dissipate. All the big tech companies have reported that they are going to substantially increase their investments in AI in 2026, so at least the demand for another year is there. It is, however, not sustainable. I cannot see a world where big tech continues to spend hundreds of billions of dollars on this tech if there is a very low return on investment. These companies are known to overspend during the growth hype years and then just as quickly tone it down to improve their bottom lines. When this happens, WDAY, which is priced essentially to perfection due to AI hype, will see massive volatility.

Even with all this AI hype, the company isn’t able to reach mid-20% top-line growth, and trading at such a forward PE ratio is not justified in my opinion. With that amount of cash on hand, the company should be even more aggressive to bring inorganic growth higher so that its top-line growth breaches 20%+.

Additionally, the SBC is still very large, at 15.5% of sales. That is a massive haircut to the company’s bottom line. Granted, it is falling, but it will continue to dilute shareholders in the near future, especially since the company looks to continue to expand.

Closing Comments

I am not interested in starting a position in WDAY, mainly because I am already heavily weighted in the AI growth story and would like to make some further adjustments in my portfolio away from AI-related stocks. That is pretty hard when you see that many companies start to rely on the hype of AI, even if there is absolutely no reason to have AI in some of these cases. The company going essentially all in on AI right now is too much of a concentration risk for now; therefore, I will be passing on it for now.

Even with the hype, the company’s share price hasn’t done well over the last 5 years, so I don’t see this changing anytime soon. Maybe 2026 will bring in some growth, but I’m sure I will find some other more promising plays.

data.title: Autodesk: Expensive For Decelerating Billings Growth (Downgrade)
data.description:
With the stock markets roaring back to all-time highs, the number-one item that should be on investors' minds is high valuations. In my view, the large-cap growth stocks that have dominated this year's gains in the market are overdue a correction, and investors should respond by rotating more of our winners into value-oriented rebound plays.

Autodesk, Inc. (ADSK) has been a rare outlier in the software industry this year. Most legacy software companies are actually in bear market territory, down more than 20% from peaks: Salesforce (CRM), Adobe (ADBE), Workday (WDAY), and HubSpot (HUBS), just to name a few. Investors are afraid that foundational AI models may begin to step in and begin offering enterprise software products: indeed, while ChatGPT is mainly a B2C company, its #2 rival Anthropic has positioned its Claude chatbot as primarily an enterprise product.

But amid this environment, Autodesk has held on to its gains. The CAD design company just reported a Q3 beat-and-raise that prompted shares higher. The question for investors is: can Autodesk hold on to these gains and rally further?

I last wrote a neutral article on Autodesk in August when the stock was trading above $310. Today, with shares of Autodesk slightly lower, I'm not tempted to enter at a slightly lower price: in my view, the company's decelerating billings growth plus a burgeoning valuation multiple position the stock as quite risky heading into 2026, especially when pessimism over legacy software names is still to catch up to this stock. I'm downgrading Autodesk to a Sell rating.

In particular, these are the red flags that I think investors should be wary of:

  • How much of Autodesk's current demand surge will be sustained? At least in the short term, Autodesk is a beneficiary of AI infrastructure spending. Corporate data center buildouts have led to greater bookings for its AECO (architecture, engineering & construction) product segment. But there's a huge likelihood that 2025 (and potentially 2026) spending levels represent pull-forwards of future capex requirements. Once we're past the rush of this investment stage, Autodesk may not be able to sustain its growth rates.
  • Current growth rates are inflated due to an accounting change. In Q4 of FY25, Autodesk updated its accounting policy for reseller contracts, putting the expense into opex instead of contra revenue. This has the impact of boosting revenue (in the current quarter, revenue growth of 18% would have been 12% under the old accounting model). We should treat Autodesk, and its valuation multiples, as a stock with low-teens rather than with high-teens growth; and later this year, this change will be fully comped, removing the growth tailwind.
  • Is Autodesk subject to potential headcount compression? Software companies like GitLab (GTLB) and Atlassian (TEAM), which primarily cater to software developers, are under pressure over fears that the rise of vibe coding may reduce the population of developers who take paid seats on their platforms. By the same vein, automation could also drive out the same engineering customer base that is Autodesk's core clientele, and threaten paid seat expansion in the long run.

Then there's a rich valuation to watch out for. At current share prices near $300, Autodesk trades at a market cap of $63.68 billion. After we net off the $2.59 billion of cash and $2.48 billion of debt on Autodesk's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $63.57 billion.

For the remainder of FY26, Autodesk has boosted its full-year revenue guidance to $7.15-$7.17 billion, or 17% y/y growth, versus 15% growth previously. The company has also boosted its pro forma EPS to a $10.21 midpoint (+20% y/y) that is 3% than a previous $9.80-$9.98 range.

This positions Autodesk's valuation multiples at:

  • 8.9x EV/FY26 revenue.
  • 28x EV/FY26 FCF.
  • 30x FY26 P/E.

We should recall that, when we adjust for Autodesk's transactional accounting changes, the company's revenue growth is really closer to the low teens, which is in line with legacy cloud software companies like Salesforce, Workday, and Adobe. I see no reason why Autodesk should trade at such a rich premium to these stocks:

In my view, Autodesk's business is neither more sticky nor more insulated from AI relative to these names. As the valuation spread has widened, I'd take the opportunity to lock in gains on Autodesk and invest instead in some of these other beaten-down software names.

Q3 Download

Let's now go through Autodesk's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

On an as-reported basis, Autodesk's revenue grew 18% y/y to $1.85 billion, beating Street expectations of $1.81 billion (+15% y/y) by a three-point margin. As a reminder, Autodesk's accounting changes for contra revenue expense against reseller-led deals is a major tailwind for revenue growth; adjusting for this change which will be fully lapped next quarter, Autodesk reports that growth would have been closer to 12% y/y.

The chart below, meanwhile, showcases Autodesk's sales by geo and product segment:

Again, one of the major drivers of revenue growth in Autodesk's largest AECO segment, which grew 23% y/y to $921 million, is AI datacenter buildouts. This may be a multi-year investment cycle, but once this tailwind dries up and datacenters are built out, Autodesk may face a dry spell.

The company notes that it's facing macro uncertainty as it closes out the calendar year, which may drive disruption for the company's sales momentum. Per CFO Janesh Moorjani's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

We have a large pool of EBA and product subscription renewals to close in the fourth quarter of the year, and we'll also have our toughest new transaction model billings and revenue growth comparisons with last year. The macroeconomic environment seems broadly stable, but macro uncertainty remains elevated, and we remain mindful of potential disruption as we continue to execute our sales and marketing optimization plan.

So we built some risk into our guidance range for the remainder of fiscal '26 and expect to again reflect these factors in our fiscal '27 outlook in February."

We note that Autodesk's billings also slowed sequentially going from Q2 to Q3. Q3 billings of 16% y/y (adjusted for currency as well as the accounting recognition update) slowed 10 points relative to 26% y/y growth in Q2.

Billings is a choppy metric that is influenced by deal timing, but it's one of the best measures we have of forward-looking revenue trajectory. Again, we don't think that Autodesk's mid/low teens' growth profile merits the company its substantial premium versus other legacy software companies, against which Autodesk's ~9x revenue multiple is currently sitting a nearly ~2x premium in some cases.

On the profitability front, pro forma operating margins improved 1 point y/y to 38%, and pro forma EPS of $2.67 grew 27% y/y and beat Street expectations of $2.50 with 7% upside.

Key Takeaways

In my view, Autodesk is sitting at a very lofty and risky valuation premium that is positioned to crumble. For some reason, Autodesk has been able to escape the pessimism that has built up around cloud software stocks that have no direct participation in the AI investment boom. While Autodesk may be seeing a temporary demand lift from datacenter buildouts, we're uncomfortable with the company's slowing billings growth and its low-teens revenue growth profile against a sharp ~9x forward revenue multiple.

Sell Autodesk and lock in gains here.

data.title: Virtus Silvant Mid-Cap Growth Fund Q3 2025 Performance Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Virtus Silvant Mid-Cap Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

Performance

The Virtus Silvant Mid-Cap Growth Fund returned +5.43% (Class INST) for the quarter, compared to the Russell Midcap Growth Index’s return of +2.78%. Information technology and financials stock selection benefited performance. Industrials stock selection weighed on returns. Palantir Technologies (PLTR) and Roblox (RBLX) were among the top stock contributors.

  • Data analytics software firm Palantir Technologies continued to outperform. Revenue growth accelerated to 48%, up from 39% last quarter. Key metrics showed the U.S. commercial segment rising 93%, up from 71%, and the U.S. government segment growing 50%, up from 45%. Margins also expanded to a record 46%, and backlog growth increased to 77%. We trimmed the position for risk control but continued to hold the stock based on its strong fundamentals.
  • Online gaming platform Roblox outperformed amid strong second-quarter results and positive guidance. Bookings exceeded consensus estimates by 12%, primarily driven by the success of the Grow a Garden game. In addition, management provided third-quarter guidance that was well above expectations and raised full-year. Daily active users remained strong, increasing by 41% percent year over year, easing concerns about market saturation. The Fund increased its position in the stock in response to these positive developments.

Fair Isaac Corp. (FICO) and Verisk Analytics (VRSK) were among the largest stock detractors.

  • Fair Isaac, best known for its FICO consumer credit score, delivered solid financial results but lagged due to regulatory pressures on its longstanding near-monopoly, particularly in the mortgage scoring market. Historically, FICO has been able to raise prices without attracting much scrutiny, but recent government efforts to increase competition have cast uncertainty over its pricing power. While the company’s underlying business remains strong, investor concern over potential erosion of its dominant position weighed on the stock. We continued to hold the position but are closely monitoring it.
  • Data analytics and risk assessment firm Verisk Analytics lagged, weighed by decelerating organic growth, a key metric, due to a below-average start to hurricane season and federal government spending-cut pressures. As a defensive stock, it also was generally out of favor in the quarter’s momentum-led market. We maintained the position, expecting these factors to be largely transitory, and overall fundamentals remain solid.
