data.title: ERC: The Foreign Currency Exposure Is Very Nice Here
data.description:
The Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund (ERC) is a closed-end fund that income-seeking investors may wish to purchase as a method of obtaining a very high level of current income from the assets that they have in their portfolios. The fund manages to do quite well in this respect, as the fund boasts a 9.34% yield at the current price. According to the fund’s website, ERC invests its assets in a variety of bonds from both domestic and foreign uses using the following rubric:

Type Of Bond

% of Total Assets

Junk Bonds

30% to 70%

Foreign Debt Securities

10% to 40%

Mortgage-Backed Securities and Investment-Grade Corporate Bonds

10% to 30%

Please note that in this case, the term “foreign debt securities” specifically refers to any debt security that is issued by an entity that is not a United States government or corporate entity. The fund may invest in emerging market debt, which would be included in the foreign debt securities category and counted against its allowable allocation to that particular sector.

Given the portfolio that the fund’s website describes, we can best compare this fund to a selection of domestic and international bond indices. When we do that, we see that the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund is far superior in terms of yield:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.83%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.58%

Vanguard Total World Bond ETF (BNDW)

4.05%

Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund ETF Shares (BNDX)

4.37%

J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index (EMB)

5.04%

The highest-yielding of these indices is the one that tracks junk bonds issued by various American entities, but even it does not come close to matching the yield of the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund. At its current yield, a $1 million investment in ERC would provide investors with an income of approximately $93,400 per year, which is approximately $7,783 per month. We should note, however, that the distribution of the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund does tend to vary from month to month:

As such, investors in this fund will not receive the exact same amount of money every month, which is rather disappointing. However, the same thing is true of the State Street SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF, which also experiences a changing distribution from month to month:

This can be overcome by an investor simply taking the distributions as cash and then withdrawing the same amount of money from their brokerage account, retirement account, or whatever account they hold their fund shares in. Over time, it should average out to allow for a relatively steady income.

The State Street SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF would only provide an annual income of $65,800, or approximately $5,483 per month, at its current price for every $1 million invested in it. This is $2,300 per month less than ERC would provide, and while everything is much more expensive today than it was a few years ago, $2,300 per month can still go a long way towards helping fund the lifestyles that most of us would like to enjoy in retirement. As such, the superior yield of the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund compared to the indices is a selling point that will likely be appealing to most income seekers.

The Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund has also proven itself to be a far superior investment vehicle than the junk bond index for anyone who is still in the process of building their wealth. We can see this by comparing the trailing ten-year total return of the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund to that of the JNK index fund:

This chart assumes that investors in each of the two assets reinvested all of the distributions that the two funds paid out into additional shares of the fund on the date that they were received. As we can see, investors in the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund received a 106.96% total return over the ten-year period, which was far superior to the 64.33% total return that investors in JNK received.

If we compare the trailing ten-year total return performance of the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund to any of the other bond index funds shown earlier, we get the same result:

As shown, an investor who owned ERC was far better off than an investor who owned any of the bond indices over the period. This should not be particularly surprising, given that the only net investment returns that bonds provide over their lifetimes are the coupon payments that they make. Thus, in many cases, the higher a bond fund’s yield, the better its total returns over any extended period of time. The exception to this is when a fund cannot cover its distributions with its investment profits and ends up depleting its net asset value. As was the case with nearly all bond funds, the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund did see its net asset value decline significantly in both 2018 and 2022:

This was due to the fact that, over the course of both of those years, interest rates were rising from levels that were very close to the zero-bound. Bonds were priced at bubble valuations prior to both of those years, so declining prices as interest rates rose to levels more appropriate to a strong economy were expected. Apart from those two years, though, we can see that the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund did a pretty good job of keeping its net asset value stable. This suggests that this fund does not overdistribute as a matter of course, but rather runs a leveraged portfolio of domestic and international bonds in an effort to earn higher yields than would be possible without the leverage. It then combines the income that it receives from its portfolio with any profits that it manages to earn through the trading of bonds and pays it out to investors. Bond index funds do not use leverage, and they do not typically try to realize capital gains by selling bonds that have gone up in price. These differences allow the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund to boast a better yield than the indices without distributing amounts that are greater than it can earn from the assets in its portfolio.

Peer Distribution Comparison

The current yield of the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund is substantially higher than that of any of the major domestic or international broad-market bond indices, as we have already seen. However, most closed-end bond funds also use leverage to boost their yields, and they also engage in bond trading to realize capital gains. Just like ERC, these funds pay out the profits that they earn from their portfolios to their shareholders. Thus, their yields are much more comparable to ERC’s yield than the index funds were. We can see that in this comparison:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund

Fixed Income – Taxable-Multi-Sector

9.34%

BlackRock Multi-Sector Income Trust (BIT)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Multi-Sector

11.31%

DoubleLine Yield Opportunities Fund (DLY)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Multi-Sector

9.70%

FS Credit Opportunities Corp. (FSCO)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Multi-Sector

13.00%

Guggenheim Strategic Opportunities Fund (GOF)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Multi-Sector

17.87%

PIMCO High Income Fund (PHK)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Multi-Sector

11.95%

TCW Strategic Income Fund (TSI)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Multi-Sector

5.32%

Virtus Global Multi-Sector Income Fund (VGI)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Multi-Sector

12.45%

Western Asset Diversified Income Fund (WDI)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Multi-Sector

12.52%

Please note that, for some reason, CEF Connect has the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund listed as a limited-duration bond fund. The fund’s fact sheet states that the average duration of ERC’s bond portfolio is 2.81 years, which is quite a bit lower than many bond funds and could allow it to be included in a comparison with other limited-duration bond funds. The fund’s website also states that ERC actively tries to limit its exposure to interest rate risk in the United States, which is a characteristic of limited-duration bond funds. However, Morningstar’s website classifies the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund as a multisector bond fund. Furthermore, the fund’s portfolio includes both domestic and foreign bonds, along with both investment-grade and speculative-grade bonds. The portfolio has much more in common with other multisector bond funds than it does with a typical limited duration bond fund. Arguably, the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund could be considered a limited-duration multisector bond fund given the fund’s stated goal to limit exposure to domestic interest rate risk, but for our purposes today, it is most appropriate to compare it with other multisector bond funds.

Unfortunately, in terms of yield, the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund does not appear to be particularly attractive when compared to its peers. The median yield out of this group of nine funds is 11.95%, which is substantially higher than the current yield of the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund. As one of the goals of many income investors is to maximize the amount of income that they earn from their portfolios, this would suggest that the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund would not be the top choice. After all, most of the peer funds shown in the table would provide their investors with a higher level of current income. Indeed, the only fund that would not provide a greater level of current income is the TCW Strategic Income Fund, which is one of the few funds in the peer group that has substantial exposure to investment-grade mortgages:

This chart comes from the semi-annual report for the TCW Strategic Income Fund. As we can see, this fund has 47.9% of its net assets invested in residential mortgage-backed securities and another 14.4% of its net assets invested in commercial mortgage-backed securities. Mortgage-backed securities tend to have lower yields than the junk bonds or emerging market securities that constitute a significant portion of the holdings of the other funds. Furthermore, the TCW Strategic Income Fund has a somewhat lower level of leverage than the other peer funds, which results in it earning somewhat lower effective yields from the bonds that it holds. As such, that fund is arguably targeting investors who are comfortable with a lower level of income in exchange for lower risks. We can see that in terms of share price performance, as TSI has only declined by 7.41% over the past ten years compared to 18.30% for ERC:

With that said, though, the lower yield of TSI does greatly reduce the amount of income that investors will receive from it. In real terms, that fund could struggle if inflation picks up going forward (as I strongly suspect will be the case). ERC is better positioned here, as its 9.34% should allow it to continue to provide investors with a respectable real yield even if inflation starts to become a problem again. This is particularly true if the shares are held in a retirement account or in some other vehicle that reduces the investor’s income tax burden.

Over the past ten years, the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund has performed admirably against its peers. This chart shows the trailing ten-year total returns of each of the peer funds that actually have a ten-year history:

The DoubleLine Yield Opportunities Fund, FS Credit Opportunities, and the Western Asset Diversified Income Fund do not have ten years of history to judge their performance, as all three of these funds have an inception date that is within the window. If we look at only the past five years, we can include the DoubleLine fund:

The DoubleLine Yield Opportunities Fund provided a 27.00% total return over the period, which was better than ERC delivered, but not nearly enough to surpass the performance of some of the other funds. Over the trailing ten-year period, only two funds – the BlackRock Multi-Sector Income Trust and the Guggenheim Strategic Opportunities Fund – managed to deliver a better performance than the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund.

Thus, despite the fund’s relatively low yield, ERC has been a reasonably solid performer out of its peer group. It has certainly not been the best performer in all of the environments that existed over the past decade, however.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund was on February 17, 2025. As such, just over nine months have passed since our last discussion on the fund. There are a lot of things that can occur in a nine-month period, and certainly, the past nine months have been incredibly eventful. There have been a number of things that affected both the bond and equity markets, many of which were not solely limited to the United States. However, the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund is exclusively a bond fund, so events that solely affected the equity markets are not really relevant to understanding what all has occurred with this fund. Thus, let us just look at the major events that impacted the bond markets over the past nine months.

One of the more significant events that occurred over the period occurred in early April when the United States imposed tariffs on many of its trading partners. The stated objective was to reduce the persistent trade deficits runs with most of the rest of the world, but it still panicked investors, and almost all asset classes sold off. The tariffs were imposed on April 2, 2025, and over the period from March 30, 2025, through April 4, 2025, the S&P 500 Index (SP500) declined 9.58%:

There were a few reasons for this, but one of the biggest ones is that the tariffs injected a great deal of uncertainty into the economy. Investors were not sure how these tariffs would affect corporate profits in the present and the future, and ultimately, stocks are valued based on the profits that investors expect the issuing company to deliver. In addition to this, analysts and economists expressed concerns that some foreign nations could impose retaliatory tariffs of their own against the United States. The fear was that a trade war might result, and that could have a major impact on the profitability of certain companies, depending on which nations were involved and the nature of the trade policies that each side imposed on the other. Furthermore, some economists expressed fears that the tariffs would lead to slowdown in global trade that could ultimately push the United States into a recession. Recessions are generally bad for cyclical companies, so this could have also impacted the performance of the equity markets.

Ordinarily, we would expect a rise in uncertainty and especially fears of a recession to be beneficial to bond prices. After all, historically, investors have sold stocks and parked money in U.S. Treasury securities whenever they got frightened. However, that is not what occurred this time. As Baytrust Financial explains:

“Starting on April 4th, Treasury bonds endured sustained selling pressure (with prices falling and yields rising) as stocks also fell, which added another layer of confusion to an already uncertain market environment. Given the U.S. Treasurys’ status as the world’s safe-haven asset during periods of global economic and market upheaval, one would have expected bond prices to rise as stocks fell. But the opposite started occurring. Investors were dumping Treasurys and stocks at the same time, with the 10-year Treasury yield notably jumping from 4.20% to over 4.50% within a four-day stretch.”

The safe-haven status that U.S. Treasuries have historically enjoyed faltered somewhat, as both stocks and bonds were selling off. This has prompted some market commentators to state that there appears to be a growing unease about the safety of U.S. Treasuries, given the U.S. government’s persistent budget deficits and apparent lack of concern from elected officials to address this. Personally, though, I doubt that there is any risk of default in nominal terms, but it is very possible that the United States will attempt to inflate away its liabilities and pay back bond investors with depreciated currency. This would represent a situation in which bonds give negative real yields. The fact that bonds sold off alongside stocks during this period could suggest that foreign investors in U.S. debt are beginning to have similar concerns, as could the fact that interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve have not been bringing down the interest rate on long-dated debt as much as might be expected.

This brings us to the second event that occurred over the past nine months, which is that the Federal Reserve resumed its attempts to ease monetary policy. This was a continuation of the central bank’s attempted monetary easing in the final four months of 2024, which saw the Federal Reserve reduce interest rates by 100 basis points over the September to December 2024 period. The continuation of the monetary easing attempts have been somewhat less aggressive, though, as so far the Federal Open Market Committee has only reduced the target federal funds range by 50 basis points:

These two 25-basis-point cuts were widely expected, and they did contribute to rising bond prices over the summer as investors attempted to front-run the Fed. As many of us can remember, the Fed’s interest rate cuts at the end of 2024 actually caused bond yields to rise as inflation made a comeback. In 2024, the yield of the ten-year U.S. Treasury note bottomed out on September 16, 2024 (the date before the first 50-basis point cut of the current easing cycle) and then rose through the end of the year. This time, the interest rate cut did not cause yields to rise too much:

U.S. Treasury Term

Yield on September 16, 2025

Current Yield

Ten-Year

4.04%

4.01%

Twenty-Year

4.61%

4.62%

Thirty-Year

4.65%

4.67%

September 16, 2025, was the day before the Federal Reserve announced the first of two 25-basis point cuts that have taken place so far in 2025. As we can see, the 50 basis points of cuts that occurred between those two dates did not have a noticeable impact on yields. However, bond yields are still lower than they were at the start of 2025, which is likely due to the market front-running the September rate cut by the Fed.

Despite the broad-market sell-off in bonds following the imposition of the tariffs in April, both investment-grade and junk bonds are higher today than they were at the start of this year:

We can, however, see the severe dip that bonds (particularly junk bonds) suffered in early April when uncertainty was causing a sell-off across the markets. Given this, and the factors discussed above, we can probably expect the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund to have delivered some share price appreciation since our last discussion, although its performance might be marred with a certain amount of volatility.

However, this is not actually what we see:

As shown, the price of both domestic investment-grade bonds and domestic junk bonds is up since the last time that we discussed this fund. However, the share price of the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund is down slightly. The fund also exhibited considerably more volatility than did either of the indices over the period, which could be a turn-off for more conservative investors. Overall, this performance is pretty disappointing.

Fortunately, the fund did a lot better when we look at total return as opposed to share price performance:

On a total return basis, the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund significantly outperformed junk bonds, and it nearly did as well as investment-grade bonds over the period. In this case, what occurred is that the distributions that the fund paid out were more than sufficient to offset the small share price decline and close the distance between its price performance and that of the indices. This is one advantage that the fund’s superior yield provides. Of course, an investor who actually took the fund’s distributions and spent them experienced a small decline in their principal, which is still disappointing no matter how we look at it.

About The Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund

The website for the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund states that the fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of current income while limiting its exposure to interest rate risk in the United States. Bonds, by their very nature, are an income vehicle, as they are both issued and redeemed at face value, so there are no net capital gains over their lifetimes. An investor who purchases a bond when it is first issued and holds it through to maturity will not earn any return except for the coupons that the bond pays out.

Of course, as was mentioned earlier, the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund is not an ordinary bond fund. It invests in a combination of both domestic and foreign bonds, and it invests in both investment-grade and speculative-grade bonds. The fund’s third-quarter 2025 holdings report states that the fund had the following asset allocation on July 31, 2025:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Agency Securities

0.09%

Asset-Backed Securities

4.58%

Common Stocks

1.14%

Corporate Bonds and Notes

63.82%

Foreign Corporate Bonds and Notes

11.28%

Foreign Government Bonds

18.55%

Loans

18.88%

Non-Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities

7.49%

Yankee Corporate Bonds and Notes

13.11%

Yankee Government Bonds

0.26%

Money Market Fund

3.54%

Interestingly, the “foreign corporate bonds and notes” category consists almost entirely of bonds issued by supranational entities backed by governments:

We see here organizations such as the Asian Development Bank, Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank, European Investment Bank, and other organizations that are ultimately funded by various national governments to achieve political objectives. Debt securities issued by these entities account for 10.95% of the fund’s net assets, nearly all of the fund’s 11.28% allocation to “foreign corporate bonds and notes.” In fact, the only bond issued in a foreign currency that was not issued by either a multinational company or a foreign government is a bond issued by Kreditanstalt fuer Wiederaufbau, which accounts for 0.33% of the fund’s net assets and is denominated in South African rand:

All the rest of the bonds that this fund holds that are denominated in a currency other than the U.S. dollar were issued by a supranational political entity or a foreign government. In total, the fund had 29.83% of its net assets invested in foreign currency-denominated bonds as of July 31, 2025.

The fact that the bulk of the foreign currency bonds held in ERC’s portfolio were issued by either a supranational political entity or a foreign government may provide a certain degree of comfort for risk-averse investors. After all, a government that issues bonds denominated in its own currency only defaults if it chooses to. All of the foreign government bonds in ERC’s portfolio are denominated in the currency of that country:

Governments have the ability to print their own currencies, so it is unlikely that any of these bonds will default in nominal terms, but it is still possible that investors might lose purchasing power over the life of the bonds if the currency declines (this is also possible with U.S. Treasury securities). We can also be fairly confident that the supranational political entities will not default on their bonds, at least nominally, due to the fact that they frequently have the ability to ask the governments that sponsored them for money to plug budget holes (through guarantees and capital calls). Thus, it is likely that there is minimal risk of defaults, at least in nominal terms, on the part of the foreign currency-denominated bonds that the fund holds.

The fund’s exposure to foreign currency bonds may have been beneficial for it since the start of the year. This chart shows the U.S. dollar index (DXY) since the start of 2025:

The U.S. dollar index measures the value of the U.S. dollar against a basket of six foreign currencies. These currencies are the euro, Japanese yen, British pound, Canadian dollar, Swedish krona, and Swiss franc. Whenever the U.S. dollar index declines, it means that the value of bonds denominated in most of these currencies increases in U.S. dollar terms. While the U.S. dollar index only specifically includes those six currencies, we have seen the U.S. dollar decline against most currencies from around the world since the start of the year. For example, here is the U.S. dollar’s year-to-date performance against the currencies that are represented in ERC’s portfolio:

Currency

YTD Performance vs. U.S. Dollar

South African rand

+9.32%

Brazilian real

+17.29%

Indian rupee

-3.86%

Colombian peso

+15.90%

Hungarian forint

+19.93%

Indonesian rupiah

-2.35%

Mexican peso

+12.40%

New Zealand dollar

+0.16%

Romanian leu

+8.97%

British pound

+5.04%

For the purpose of understanding this chart, a positive value means that the U.S. dollar declined against the currency in question, whereas a negative value means that the U.S. dollar rose in value against the foreign currency. This also means that an American investor who is holding a bond denominated in the foreign currency would have seen the bond rise in price for all of the currencies with positive values. As we can see, the only currencies against which the U.S. dollar declined in value were the Indian rupee and the Indonesian rupiah. All of the others gained in U.S. dollar terms. The fact that ERC has nearly 30% of its net assets invested in bonds denominated in these foreign currencies could thus give it a real advantage over a fund whose assets are entirely denominated in U.S. dollars.

Of course, this is backward-looking, and an investor buying this fund today would not benefit from the gains that these currencies have delivered year-to-date. In order to benefit from the presence of the foreign currency-denominated bonds in the fund’s portfolio, the U.S. dollar would have to continue to decline against these currencies. I suspect that this will be the case, at least against the emerging market currencies. The United States and most other developed countries have sizable structural government deficits that are driven by the needs of their aging populations. It is likely that we are entering an environment known as “financial repression,” which refers to a situation where interest rates are kept lower than would ordinarily be the case in order to allow the government to borrow cheaply and minimize carrying costs on the national debt, and the central bank engages in the buying of government securities to support government deficit spending. In short, the government employs inflation as a way to reduce the real costs of carrying its debt and supporting its spending activities. Many emerging market nations have more favorable demographics and fiscal profiles, along with export-driven economies with substantial manufacturing bases. This seems likely to result in emerging market currencies outperforming developed ones over the long term, although this thesis could potentially be weakened if these nations deliberately try and weaken their currencies in order to export to the United States and Europe. Personally, I suspect that foreign currency-denominated emerging market bonds will probably prove to be the best place for fixed-income investors going forward, and it is nice to see that ERC is maintaining a position in these assets.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund is to provide its investors with a high level of current income. In accordance with this objective, the fund pays a monthly distribution to its investors. The most recent distribution is $0.0722 per share ($0.8664 per share annualized), but this distribution has changed a few times over the past year. This is in accordance with the fund’s managed distribution plan, which sets the distribution at 8.75% of the average net asset value over the preceding twelve-month period. As such, the fund’s distribution varies with its net asset value and does not remain consistent over the long term. This may, admittedly, reduce the appeal of this fund for those who are looking for a safe and secure income from their portfolios. This managed distribution does essentially require the fund to earn an annual return of at least 8.75% in order to keep the net asset value stable, so let us have a look at the fund’s finances to see how well it is achieving that goal.

As of the time of writing, the most recent financial report that is available for the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund is the semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on April 30, 2025. While this report is several months old at present, it is still the newest one that is currently available, and it is newer than the one that we had available the last time that we looked at this fund, so let us review it for an update.

For the six-month period that ended on April 30, 2025, the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund reported a net investment income of $10,126,036. This was not sufficient to cover the $12,064,257 that the fund paid out in distributions over the same period.

Unfortunately, this fund failed to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the six-month period that ended on April 30, 2025, ERC reported net realized losses of $2,949,812. The fund had an additional $525,012 in net unrealized losses. Thus, the investment portfolio only generated $6,651,212 over the six-month period:

This was obviously not sufficient to cover the distribution over the period. However, the fund did manage to fully cover the distribution for the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2024, and it had a great deal of excess investment profits to help support the distribution in the most recent reporting period. If we look at the past eighteen months, the fund covered all of the distributions that were paid out and still saw its net asset value increase by $10,372,999. Thus, for now, the distribution appears to be reasonably secure, but it would still be wise to keep an eye on the fund’s NAV in order to make sure that the distribution remains secure.

Valuation

Shares of ERC are currently trading at a 6.32% discount on net asset value. This is reasonably in line with the 6.07% discount on net asset value that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month.

The fund’s current price is quite attractive compared to its long-term historical pricing. Over the past five years, the shares of this fund have traded at prices ranging from a 10.77% discount to a 9.40% premium on net asset value. The average price over the five-year period has been a 4.27% discount on net asset value, which is a less attractive price than the fund has today.

Here is how the current price of the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund compares with that of its peers:

The median price of this group of nine funds is the 5.17% discount of the TCW Strategic Income Fund. That fund currently has a smaller discount than the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund, which tells us that ERC is not particularly expensive relative to its peer group. When we also consider that the fund is trading at a price that is better than its long-term historical price, the current price looks reasonable as an entry point.

Expenses

The semi-annual report states that the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund had an annualized expense ratio of 3.27% for the six-month period that ended on April 30, 2025. If we exclude the expenses that the fund had due to its use of leverage, its expense ratio drops to 1.00%.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund appears to be a reasonable way to add bond exposure to a portfolio, if such exposure is even desired. Bonds in general run the risk of delivering negative real returns going forward, but the low duration of ERC should mitigate this somewhat. The fund also has exposure to foreign currency-denominated bonds that should add some protection in the event that the U.S. dollar declines against foreign currencies. Personally, I am hesitant to recommend any bonds denominated in a fiat currency due to the potential for all countries to devalue their currency in an attempt to protect their exports as the U.S. dollar and other developed countries come under fiscal stress going forward.

data.title: The 'Real' Economy Is In Real Trouble
data.description:
The massive surge in tech spending with the AI infrastructure buildout continues to power the U.S. economy. This has led to a quite bifurcated economic landscape. I am struggling to remember another time where there was such a disconnect between the headline GDP numbers and the "real" economy.

After a slight contraction in the first quarter, largely due to companies front-loading imports to avoid coming tariffs, GDP growth in Q2 came in at a robust 3.9%. The most recent forecast from the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow has 4.2% GDP growth penciled in for Q3.

Of course, a huge portion of this GDP growth is being driven by the huge surge of tech spending for AI infrastructure. The capital expenditure budget at Amazon (AMZN) in FY2026 will be roughly three times that of FY2023. Other tech giants like Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta Platforms (META) are making similar financial commitments.

Relative to GDP, spending on AI has already surpassed the peak of the Internet Boom. We are not to the peak of the railroad boom from 150 years ago yet. However, from a depreciation basis, that has been achieved as railroad components (track, engines, boxcars, etc..) have a much longer useful life than a GPU. And remember that previous massive misallocation of capital led directly to the Panic of 1873 and more than four years of recession.

The huge amount of fiscal deficit spending is also contributing to GDP growth. However, given the U.S.'s debt-to-GDP ratio, how long this is sustainable is an open question. The military industrial complex is doing well thanks to the proxy war in Ukraine and the huge boost in defense spending in the FY2026 federal budget.

Outside of these areas, it is difficult to find many economic engines that are not sputtering right now, as the "real" economy is either in a recession or getting close to it. Housing-related activity makes up 15% to 18% of GDP. As I recently highlighted, the pain in the moribund housing sector is just getting started, and 2026 is shaping up to be a hard year for home sellers.

Housing affordability remains historically low, and average mortgage rates remain stubbornly above the 6% threshold despite 125bps worth of reductions to the Fed Funds rate since the summer of 2024. 2026 is about to commence with the number of home sellers over home buyers at their highest levels in more than a decade. A reading this week from Zillow shows that the average home listing experienced record-breaking cumulative discounts of up to $25,000 in October.

Then we have the commercial real estate or CRE. Multi-family and the Office sectors are really struggling right now. These account for approximately $3.5 trillion of the roughly $4.8 trillion in CRE debt outstanding. The delinquency rate for Commercial Mortgage Back Securities or CMBS on office properties has surpassed the peak of the Great Financial Crisis and is close to 12%. And this does not account for the considerable amount of 'extend & pretend' loan modifications that have taken place by lenders.

CMBS delinquency rates around multi-family (around $2.2 trillion in outstanding debt) have just more than doubled over the past 13-14 months to near 7%. With median rents flatlining nationally and falling in many major metro regions, while costs (insurance, maintenance, taxes, staffing, etc.) continue to rise at least at the pace of overall inflation, the situation here is going to get worse before it gets better.

Then we have the jobs market, which clearly has started to deteriorate in recent months as I documented again in an article earlier this week. Major companies like United Parcel Service (UPS) and Amazon have announced significant new layoffs recently, and October had the largest number (153K) of layoff announcements of any October since 2003. Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE) just announced it would lay off approximately 10% of its employee base in the coming quarters.

Despite the huge surge of spending on data center construction and infrastructure, it hasn't been enough to trigger a rise in the U.S. Leading Economic Indicators Index, which continues to decline despite the robust quarterly GDP figures.

Today, the Chicago PMI reading came in at 36.3 in November, from 43.8 in October. This is compared with the 44.3 consensus. Anything below a 50 reading is recessionary, it should be noted.

Then we have the state of the consumer, which drives roughly two-thirds of GDP growth. Even with recent 4% headline GDP growth, a recent survey showed roughly one-quarter of all Americans are living paycheck to paycheck.

Sub-prime auto loans 60 days or more past due hit 6.65% in October. That is a higher delinquency rate than the peak seen in the Great Financial Crisis. Student loan payments and collections were reinstated earlier this year, after a nearly four-year taxpayer funded hiatus. Not surprisingly, student loan delinquency rates are soaring. Nearly 13% of the just under $1.8 billion in student loan debt is now seriously past due.

Student loans are also now again being reported to credit bureaus, significantly lowering credit scores for millions of student loan holders. This depletes the potential buying pool for big ticket items like homes, autos, appliances, boats, and RVs.

Finally, total credit card debt continues to rise. It hit an all-time high of $1.33 trillion in October. Given the faltering jobs and housing market, debt loads, and rising delinquencies, is it any wonder consumer sentiment is in the tank?

This is the $64,000 question for investors. Can the market continue to trade at extreme valuation levels viewed from several historical valuation metrics when the economy is primarily being held up by the AI Revolution and much of the "real" economy is in recession or close to being so?

This is something we are going to find out together as we get into 2026.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
